clearing_status.fact: SEC granted a formal one-year extension on Feb 25, 2025 (effective Mar 4, 2025), shifting cash deadline to Dec 31, 2026 and repo to Jun 30, 2027 (SEC Release No. 34-102487; sec.gov press release Feb 25, 2025). As of Apr-May 2026, two exemptive-relief requests remain pending: SIFMA inter-affiliate (SEC notice Apr 17, 2026, Release 34-105262) and IIB non-U.S. transactions (comment period reopened Apr 17, 2026, Release 34-105261), with comment letters filed through May 29, 2026. No second extension has been announced (SEC featured topic page, last updated referencing Apr 20, 2026 Uyeda statement; TBAC Charge Q2 2026 dated May 29, 2026).
clearing_status.rationale: The rubric scores 1 if 'an official extension [has been] granted, OR exemptive-relief requests pending.' Both conditions are met: a formal one-year extension was granted in Feb 2025, and multiple scope-narrowing exemptive-relief petitions (SIFMA, IIB) are actively pending before the SEC as of July 2026. No second extension exists, so rubric = 2 is not triggered.

plan_financing.fact: Japan 10Y JGB yield climbed toward 2.8% as of ~July 3-4 2026, 'approaching its highest level since October 1996 after a weak 10-year JGB auction [July 2] reinforced concerns over rising fiscal spending and increased government borrowing'; yield up ~130 bps over 12 months and ~55-60 bps from the ~2.2% range prevailing before the latest fiscal strategy announcement (Source: TradingEconomics/Reuters, July 3-4 2026; MOF auction result July 2 2026).
plan_financing.rationale: The >50bps yield spike on fiscal concern (10Y from ~2.2% to ~2.75-2.8%) meets the rubric's disorderly threshold; the July 2 auction was explicitly described as 'weak', reinforcing — not contradicting — the distress signal, even though a confirmed BTC<2 is not in hand.

dissent.view: May 25 CNBC reported Takaichi's ¥3 trillion supplementary budget specified no extra bond issuance to reassure markets, and prior auctions (BTC ~3.02 in March) were still clearing; some argue the yield rise reflects BOJ normalization rather than pure fiscal panic.

direct_lending_default.fact: Fitch Ratings reported its U.S. Private Credit Default Rate hit a record 6.0% for the 12 months ended April 2026 (reported May 18, 2026), and remained at 6.0% in May 2026 — the highest since Fitch launched the index in August 2024. Source: Fitch Ratings via Bloomberg Tax, CNBC, ZeroHedge (May 2026).
direct_lending_default.rationale: The rubric calls for the latest direct-lending/private-credit default rate as a percent; Fitch's index (monitoring ~1,500 private credit issuers) is the most granular, most recent, and most widely cited primary data point, placing the trailing-12M rate at 6.0% as of May 2026. Corroborating signals — surging PIK income, BDC NAV writedowns, redemption gates at Blackstone/Ares/Partners Group, and elevated Treasury yields forcing costlier refinancings — all independently validate an elevated and rising default environment.

dissent.view: Proskauer Private Credit Default Index (697 loans, $189.2B): 2.73% default rate in Q1 2026, up from 1.84% two quarters prior — lower because it uses a narrower, strict-payment-default methodology.
Moody's Analytics approximates the 2025 private credit default rate at 1.6%–4.7%, with the wide range reflecting exclusion vs. inclusion of distressed exchanges/restructurings.
Fitch (broader methodology, trailing 12M through Jan 2026): 5.8% — the highest at that time; 9.2% estimated for private-credit-backed corporate borrowers in full-year 2025.
Morgan Stanley warns default rate could surge toward 8% in 2026, driven by AI disruption of software sector (26% of direct-lending portfolios); characterized as 'significant but not systemic'.

redemption_gates.fact: In Q2 2026, Apollo Debt Solutions BDC capped repurchases at 5% against 16.8% requests (~$2.4B); BCRED prorated to its 5% cap after ~10% requests on its ~$82.5B fund; Blue Owl OCIC maintained its 5% cap on $3.6B in requests; and per PitchBook/Investing.com (late June 2026), Ares, Morgan Stanley, HPS, Cliffwater, and Monroe BDCs all hit their 5% quarterly repurchase caps and prorated investor redemptions. Combined AUM of capped funds far exceeds $100B. Sources: AltsWire (June 22, 2026); Investing.com (July 2, 2026); PitchBook/BofA (April 29, 2026).
redemption_gates.rationale: Seven or more named non-traded BDCs — including the three largest by AUM (BCRED ~$82.5B, Apollo Debt Solutions ~$16.8B NAV, Blue Owl OCIC) — had their 5% quarterly repurchase caps actively binding with forced pro-rata payouts in Q2 2026, satisfying both the >=3 largest funds criterion and the combined AUM >$100B criterion for WIDESPREAD (score=2). Stanger notes no fund has fully suspended repurchases, but the rubric explicitly covers 'quarterly repurchase caps active,' which are unambiguously in force.

dissent.view: Stanger (May 14, 2026) states 'no NAV BDC has gated redemptions' — arguing the 5% cap is a structural feature functioning as designed, not an emergency gate, and that sponsors delivered record liquidity in Q1 2026.

new_imf_programs.fact: IMF news page (imf.org/en/news, accessed 2026-07-06) shows on June 24, 2026 the Board 'Approves 42-month Arrangements under Extended Credit Facility (ECF) and the Extended Fund Facility (EFF)' — a newly approved arrangement. Most other Jan-Jul 2026 IMF actions are reviews or augmentations of existing programs (Argentina, Egypt, Pakistan, DRC, Burkina Faso, Ghana PCI transition). No confirmed EM sovereign missed coupon or hard-default event found in Jan-Jul 2026 in IMF, S&P, or financial press sources.
new_imf_programs.rationale: At least one genuinely new IMF arrangement was Board-approved in the trailing 6 months (June 24, 2026 42-month ECF/EFF), with no confirmed sovereign missed coupon/default in the same window; this is consistent with 1-2 new arrangements and zero missed coupons, mapping to RISING (1) under the rubric.

dissent.view: Most Jan-Jul 2026 IMF actions are reviews/augmentations of pre-existing programs rather than first-time distress-driven arrangements; if those are excluded, the new-program count may fall to 1 or even 0, potentially warranting CALM (0).

china_ppi.fact: In May 2026, China's producer price index for industrial products (PPI) increased by 3.9% year on year. Source: NBS press release https://www.stats.gov.cn/english/PressRelease/202606/t20260611_1963929.html, released 2026-06-11.
china_ppi.rationale: The +3.9% YoY print is the highest since July 2022 and constitutes a sharp positive move; per the rubric, a large move in either direction is a price-spillover shock — here a cost-push inflation signal that exports input-cost pressure globally. The June 2026 NBS release (covering June data) is not yet published as of 2026-07-06.

trade_actions.fact: USTR (ustr.gov, June 2 2026) proposed 12.5% Section 301 tariffs on China under the forced-labor investigation (60 economies); separately USTR initiated a Section 301 excess-capacity investigation targeting China on March 11 2026; two Section 232 proclamations modified steel/aluminum/copper tariffs affecting China (April 2 and June 1 2026); and multiple AD/CVD preliminary determinations on Chinese goods (van-type trailers, solar PV cells, fiberglass door panels) were published in the Federal Register in June 2026 -- totalling >=4 distinct new measures in the trailing 3 months, with the proposed 12.5% broad tariff round covering virtually all Chinese goods. China's MOFCOM condemned the June 2 action as violating the Beijing summit consensus and warned of 'resolute and forceful retaliation' (China Briefing, June 13 2026), though no counter-measures had been formally announced as of July 6.
trade_actions.rationale: The rubric threshold for ESCALATING is >=4 new measures OR a broad tariff round >=10% across a sector. Both conditions are met: at least 6 distinct actions (two Section 301 investigations, two Section 232 proclamations, multiple AD/CVD findings, BIOSECURE entity-list addition) were taken against China in April-July 2026, and the proposed 12.5% Section 301 covers all Chinese goods (broad sector tariff). China's public condemnation of the June 2 action as a truce violation further supports an escalating rather than managed-truce read.

dissent.view: The November 2025 one-year truce (reciprocal tariff suspended through Nov 10 2026) remains formally intact, and the 12.5% forced-labor tariff is still proposed-not-final (hearings July 7 2026); one could read the environment as RISING (managed friction) rather than full ESCALATING until the tariff is finalized.

license_flow.fact: China's April 2025 MOFCOM Announcement No. 18 licensing controls on seven heavy REEs (incl. dysprosium, terbium, yttrium) remain fully active; the Jan 1 2026 Export Licensing Catalogue added samarium, gadolinium, and lutetium compounds (new categories within trailing 3 months); on June 22 2026 MOFCOM added MP Materials and USA Rare Earth to its export-control entity list; formal review window is 45 working days (~9 weeks) with actual delays 'often exceeding three months' (Dura Magnetics/MOFCOM via Clark Hill); licensing is case-by-case/whitelist for named end-users, not blanket — general licences issued only to a handful of pre-cleared large Chinese magnet makers (JL MAG, San Huan, Yunsheng, Dec 2025); defense/aerospace applications face categorical denial per MOFCOM. Sources: MOFCOM Announcement No. 18 (Apr 2025); MOFCOM/Global Times press briefing Jun 26 2025; Andersen Institute Apr 29 2026; CSIS May 5 2026; ORF Jul 4 2026.
license_flow.rationale: The regime satisfies at least three independent Rubric-1 triggers simultaneously: (a) whitelist/case-by-case licensing rather than blanket approvals; (b) processing times of 45 working days officially and 3+ months in practice, far exceeding the 30-day threshold; (c) new categories (samarium, gadolinium, lutetium) added to the control list on Jan 1 2026, within the trailing 3-month window. Rubric-2 is not warranted because civilian commercial licences continue to be issued and May 2026 export volumes rose 23% MoM, meaning no categorical freeze is in effect. [cross-check (asi1): two reads disagreed (1 vs 0); held for review]

truce_status.fact: The Nov-2025 Busan truce (effective Nov 10, 2025, nominal expiry Nov 10, 2026) suspended China's October 2025 rare-earth export controls for one year; however, China's April 2025 controls on heavy REEs (dysprosium, terbium, yttrium, scandium) were explicitly never suspended and remain fully active. Chinese customs data through May 2026 shows heavy REE exports to the US still ~50% below pre-control levels; CSIS (May 2026) records only 17 tons of yttrium exported to the US in the 8 months after April 2025 vs. 333 tons prior. A U.S. official (E&E News, May 12, 2026) confirmed the deal is 'still in effect' but said truce extension timing is 'not clear yet.' White House May 2026 summit fact sheet still lists rare earth shortages as an unresolved U.S. concern. Primary sources: White House Fact Sheet Nov 5 2025 (whitehouse.gov); CSIS 'Rare Earth Export Restrictions One Year Later' May 2026 (csis.org); E&E News May 12, 2026; Morrison Foerster Nov 13, 2025 (mofo.com); Mining Weekly May 12, 2026.
truce_status.rationale: The rubric's 'partial breach' prong under score 1 is triggered: the April 2025 export controls on heavy REEs were excluded from the truce and remain fully active, with volume data confirming a ~50% suppression of heavy REE exports — a material, corroborated supply disruption not covered by the truce's suspension. The <90-day clock (expiry Nov 10, 2026) has not yet been reached as of July 6, 2026 (~127 days remaining), so the time-based prong alone does not yet fire, but the partial breach prong independently yields a score of 1.

re_blacklist.fact: On 22 June 2026, China's MOFCOM issued Announcement No. 23 of 2026, placing 10 named US entities on its export control list under the Export Control Law and Dual-Use Item Regulations. The two US rare-earth producers explicitly named are MP Materials Corp. and USA Rare Earth, Inc. The action bars Chinese exporters from supplying dual-use items to those firms and prohibits any party globally from transferring Chinese-origin dual-use goods to them. Sources: Al Jazeera (2026-06-22), Benchmark Minerals (2026-06-22), metal.com/SMM (2026-06-23), ORF Online (2026-07-04).
re_blacklist.rationale: Two named US rare-earth producers (MP Materials Corp. and USA Rare Earth, Inc.) are explicitly listed by MOFCOM as of 22 June 2026, satisfying rubric level 1 (entity-specific). Multiple corroborating sources and the muted share-price reaction confirm the restriction is firm-specific, not a categorical sector-wide ban, ruling out level 2.

hree_premium.fact: Benchmark Mineral Intelligence (March 24, 2026) reports dysprosium oxide CIF North America was 4.4x EXW China in 2025, with its Rare Earths Service forecasting ~8.3x by 2027; Oregon Group (June 2026) citing Argus data puts ex-China dysprosium oxide at ~$1,450/kg vs SMM domestic ~$208/kg, implying ~7x. Ucore (Feb 23, 2026 Newsfile) cited ~5x for dysprosium oxide. Source: benchmarkminerals.com, March 24 2026; oregongroup.com citing Argus, ~June 2026.
hree_premium.rationale: The Benchmark 4.4x reading is the most methodologically explicit primary-source figure (CIF N. America vs EXW China, dysprosium oxide); the Argus-cited ~7x for June 2026 and the Benchmark forecast trajectory of ~8.3x by 2027 indicate the multiple has risen materially through H1 2026, placing the mid-2026 central estimate near 4.5-5x on a conservative basis and up to 7x on spot transaction evidence.

genius_impl.fact: As of July 6 2026 — 12 days before the statutory July 18 2026 deadline — ALL GENIUS Act implementing rules remain NPRMs, not final rules. OCC NPRM published Mar 2 2026 (comments closed May 1); FDIC NPRM published Apr 10 2026 (comments closed Jun 9); Treasury/FinCEN/OFAC AML NPRM published Apr 9 2026 (comments closed Jun 9). The Federal Reserve has issued NO prudential proposed rule for PPSIs under its supervision. Sources: OCC Bulletin 2026-3 (occ.treas.gov, Feb 25 2026); FDIC FIL (fdic.gov, Apr 7 2026); Treasury SB0435 (treasury.gov, Apr 9 2026); Freshfields blog (Apr 14 2026); American Prospect (Jun 24 2026, citing Fed absence).
genius_impl.rationale: All rules are in proposed/comment stage as the July 18 deadline looms — a direct rubric-1 match ('rules still in proposed/comment stage near the deadline'). The Federal Reserve's failure to issue any prudential NPRM constitutes a documented inter-agency gap, further satisfying rubric-1's 'inter-agency divergence' criterion; no court stay or compliance failure justifies rubric-2.

dissent.view: Some industry trackers (angelinvestorsnetwork.com, cryptoimpacthub.com) describe agencies as 'finalizing' rules and frame the sprint as on-track, implying a possible rubric-0 outcome if agencies rush final rules before Jul 18; however no confirmed final rules have been published.

ai_revenue_gap.fact: Q1 2026 earnings: combined hyperscaler 2026 capex ~$725B, up ~77% YoY (MSFT +84% QoQ capex to $30.88B; Alphabet capex $35.67B, >2x YoY; Amazon capex $44.2B). AI-attributable revenue: Google Cloud +63% YoY to $20B, MSFT AI run-rate $37B +123% YoY, AWS +28% YoY. Allianz Research (March 2026) and Forbes/CreditSights (June 2026) confirm capex growing ~50% faster than AI revenue, a ~46% divergence. Sources: Q1 2026 earnings releases via Fortune (2026-04-30), Financial Times/Tom's Hardware (2026-04-30), Forbes/CreditSights (2026-06-02).
ai_revenue_gap.rationale: Capex growth (~77-84% YoY) materially exceeds AI-revenue growth (~28-63% YoY), satisfying the WIDE condition. However, AI revenue is still strongly accelerating YoY at all major hyperscalers with no confirmed QoQ deceleration in Q1 2026, so the WIDENING FAST threshold (score 2) is not met.

dissent.view: Jefferies analyst Brent Thill argued 'The bear thesis is garbage' and that revenue growth justifies capex outlays; Alphabet and MSFT traded higher post-earnings, suggesting some market participants view the gap as closing at Alphabet specifically.

Allianz Research and Forbes/CreditSights characterise the 46% capex-vs-revenue divergence as already exceeding the 2001 telecom overbuild threshold, implying risk closer to a score of 2 on systemic grounds.

gpu_refi_stress.fact: FT reported May 3 2026 that JPMorgan, Morgan Stanley and SMBC are seeking to offload portions of AI data-center debt at a discount as exposure hits internal limits; CoreWeave carried a named $4.2B refinancing wall due in 2026 on GPU-collateralized debt (davefriedman.substack.com, April 2026; resultsense.com citing FT, May 6 2026). However, CoreWeave successfully closed $8.5B DDTL 4.0 (investment-grade, Moody's A3) on March 31 2026 and a $3.1B broadly-syndicated loan in May 2026 drew $19B in orders; its 5yr CDS fell from 8.81% in December 2025 to 4.52% by June 10 2026 (Bloomberg). No confirmed default or failed refinancing of GPU-backed paper has occurred.
gpu_refi_stress.rationale: Banks offloading data-center debt at a discount and a named borrower (CoreWeave) facing a $4.2B 2026 refinancing wall satisfy rubric Level 1 (SIGNS); however, CoreWeave executed successful refinancings and spreads tightened materially, and no confirmed default, distressed exchange, or failed refinancing exists to justify Level 2.

cmbs_office_delinq.fact: Trepp CMBS office delinquency rate edged up four bps to 11.57% in June 2026 (Connect CRE, citing Trepp, published ~July 1 2026).
cmbs_office_delinq.rationale: The rubric requires the latest published CMBS office delinquency rate as a percent; Trepp's June 2026 reading of 11.57% is the most recent authoritative figure available and maps directly to the rubric. The rate has oscillated in the 11-12% range since January 2026's all-time high of 12.34%, confirming sustained, systemic stress in office CMBS rather than an idiosyncratic event.

regional_bank_stress.fact: FDIC failed-bank list (last updated May 1, 2026) shows exactly 2 US bank failures YTD 2026: Metropolitan Capital Bank & Trust (Chicago, $261M assets, Jan 30 2026) and Community Bank and Trust - West Georgia (LaGrange, $288M assets, May 1 2026). Both are under $10bn. Bisnow (Feb 2, 2026) reported Metropolitan had CRE/real-estate loan exposure (Rosewood skilled nursing facility portfolio) as a contributing factor; regulators did not formally cite CRE concentration. The Fed issued a formal enforcement action against Community Bankshares Inc. (April 2026) citing board oversight, capital, and affiliate-transaction compliance - not CRE concentration. No third failure or interagency CRE-emergency action found. Source: https://www.fdic.gov/bank-failures/failed-bank-list (May 1, 2026); https://www.bisnow.com/national/news/capital-markets/cre-tied-to-first-bank-failure-of-2026-133023; https://www.bankingdive.com/news/georgia-bank-failure-fdic-anchor-florida/819186/
regional_bank_stress.rationale: Two failures in the trailing 6 months, each well under $10bn assets, maps directly to the ISOLATED (1) rubric threshold of '1-2 such failures each <$10bn assets'; the CRE/real-estate link for Metropolitan is confirmed by credible reporting (Bisnow) and the West Georgia failure was preceded by a Fed public enforcement action, satisfying at least the 'OR >=1 new public enforcement action' prong. No evidence of a third failure or coordinated systemic CRE action that would trigger CLUSTER (2). [cross-check (asi1): two reads disagreed (1 vs 0); held for review]

govt_stability.fact: PM Sébastien Lecornu's minority government (Lecornu II, France's 48th government) survived two no-confidence motions on 2 Feb 2026 (260 and 135 votes vs. 289 needed) after invoking Article 49.3 to pass the 2026 budget; it remains in office as of early July 2026 with no dissolution or snap election called. Sources: Al Jazeera 2 Feb 2026 (https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2026/2/2/france-adopts-2026-budget-after-two-no-confidence-votes-fail); Euronews 23 Jan 2026 (https://www.euronews.com/2026/01/23/french-government-avoids-collapse-after-surviving-two-no-confidence-votes); Wikipedia 'Lecornu governments' (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lecornu_governments).
govt_stability.rationale: Lecornu governs a hung parliament without a working majority, has repeatedly invoked Article 49.3, and has survived multiple no-confidence votes through ad-hoc Socialist support — the textbook Rubric 1 (FRAGILE) condition. No confidence vote has succeeded and no dissolution/snap election has been called, ruling out Rubric 2.

rating_action.fact: S&P cut France long-term sovereign rating from AA- to A+ on 17 Oct 2025 (stable outlook); Fitch cut from AA- to A+ on 12 Sep 2025; Moody's affirmed Aa3 but revised outlook to negative on 24 Oct 2025. Sources: spglobal.com (article dated 17 Oct 2025), fitchratings.com via internationalbanker.com (Sep 12 2025), ratings.moodys.com (Oct 24 2025).
rating_action.rationale: Two of the Big Three agencies (Fitch on 12 Sep 2025 and S&P on 17 Oct 2025) executed actual one-notch rating cuts on France within the trailing 12 months from 2026-07-06, satisfying rubric band 2. Moody's negative outlook (band 1) is subsumed by the more severe band 2 triggered by the executed downgrades.

subprime_auto_dpd.fact: Fitch Ratings subprime auto ABS 60+ day delinquency rate: 6.90% in January 2026 (record high), edging down to 6.80% in February 2026 — per Wolf Street (May 19, 2026) citing Fitch Ratings monthly index data.
subprime_auto_dpd.rationale: The Fitch subprime auto ABS 60+ DPD index is the standard authoritative benchmark; 6.80% (February 2026) is the latest published monthly figure and maps directly to the rubric. S&P Global reported a full-year 2025 average of 6.18%, confirming the elevated regime but reflecting a different aggregation period.

dissent.view: S&P Global Ratings confirmed full-year 2025 subprime auto ABS 60+ DPD reached a record 6.18% (annual average, not a monthly peak), described as 'at an all-time high' — slightly lower than the Fitch January/February 2026 monthly readings but consistent directionally.

bnpl_delinq.fact: Affirm FQ3'26 Shareholder Letter (SEC Form 8-K, filed ~May 2026): 30+ day delinquency rate (monthly installment loans, ex-Peloton/Pay-in-X) was 2.8% as of March 31, 2026, up 29 basis points YoY from 2.5% in FQ3'25; allowance for credit losses was 6.0% of loans held for investment, up from 5.7% YoY; recent cohort net charge-offs tracking ~3.5% of cohort GMV. Source: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/0001820953/000162828026032105/affirmfq326shareholderle.htm
bnpl_delinq.rationale: 30+ DPD rose ~29bps YoY (well under the 100bps threshold for level 2) and cohort NCOs are tracking ~3.5% of GMV - above the 3% charge-off threshold but this is a cohort-lifetime NCO estimate (not a period charge-off rate) and is described as in-line with historical expectations, so the rubric's 'contained (<3%)' charge-off criterion is borderline but not clearly breached at the period level; the data maps cleanly to level 1: rising delinquency YoY with charge-offs not yet spiking systemically.

dissent.view: Statista notes Affirm 30+ DPD increased in Q2 2026 (calendar) vs same quarter 2025 by an undisclosed number of basis points, but characterises it as 'still lower than previous peaks such as 2020', suggesting a modest rise consistent with level 1 rather than level 2.

dollar_reserve_share.fact: IMF COFER Data Brief (published ~March 27, 2026): 'The share of US dollar holdings decreased to 56.77 percent in 2025Q4, from 56.93 percent in 2025Q3.' Total FX reserves = $13.14 trillion. Source: https://data.imf.org/en/news/imf%20data%20brief%20march%2027
dollar_reserve_share.rationale: The rubric asks for the latest USD share of global FX reserves as reported by IMF COFER; the most recent published quarter is 2025Q4 at 56.77%, released ~27 March 2026 with ~3-month publication lag meaning Q1 2026 data is not yet available. The figure maps directly to the rubric with no transformation required.

dissent.view: BestBrokers (citing pre-methodology-revision IMF data) reported 56.32% as of 2025Q2 under the old 'allocated reserves' framework, which excluded unallocated reserves; this is an older, superseded series and lower observation window.

weaponization_event.fact: BRICS Pay began operational deployment in 2026 with full launch targeted for the India BRICS summit (Aug/Sep 2026), per bricspayment.ru citing Russian FM Lavrov at the Rio summit; a gold-backed 'Unit' token pilot launched Oct 2025. No new sovereign reserve seizure has been executed: the ~$280-300B Russian CBR freeze (since Feb 2022) was re-characterised under EU Article 122 as indefinite, and Russia obtained a Moscow court judgment (May 26 2026) ordering Euroclear to pay ~$232B, with Euroclear countersuing in Brussels (first hearing June 25 2026) — all litigation, no new state-on-state seizure of a different sovereign's reserves. Sources: bricspayment.ru (2026), tftc.io (July 2026), brookings.edu (June 2025), kyivpost.com (Mar 2026).
weaponization_event.rationale: BRICS Pay operational deployment and the Unit gold-backed token pilot constitute new non-dollar settlement agreements/launches meeting Band 1; the EU Article 122 permanent freeze is escalation of an existing 2022 freeze, not a new sovereign seizure, and the Russia-Euroclear litigation is unresolved legal dispute, not executed action — insufficient for Band 2.

dissent.view: Some sources (informedclearly.com, watcher.guru) characterise BRICS Pay as already 'live' and fully operational in 2026 with intra-bloc USD usage cut by two-thirds, which could push toward Band 2 on settlement shift grounds
EU's invocation of Article 122 to make the Russian asset freeze indefinite could be read as a new form of sovereign asset seizure action rather than mere re-characterisation of an existing freeze

insurer_exits.fact: A June 2026 analysis (climatecosmos.com) identifies 13 states as ground-zero for 2026 non-renewal waves (CA, FL, TX, GA, OK, NE, MN, CO, NC, LA, KS, IA, and others); State Farm newsroom (May 4, 2026) confirms most of CA's top-12 homeowners insurers have paused or limited new policies since 2022; however, AM Best's 2026 Market Segment Outlook Report (cited HousingWire, June 2, 2026) projected stabilization across the US homeowners segment, Florida's Citizens received an 8.7% rate decrease, and no top-10 insurer has executed a full state exit in 2026.
insurer_exits.rationale: Documented non-renewal wave states number approximately 13, placing the indicator squarely in the rubric's RISING band (5-15 states elevated, some markets stabilizing). The ACCELERATING threshold (>15 states or a top-10 insurer fully exiting a major state) is not met: Florida is actively stabilizing, Farmers re-entered CA, and AM Best's sector outlook moved to 'stable' in December 2025.

dissent.view: climatecosmos.com (June 2026) frames the 13-state count as 'accelerating, not reversing,' implying the trajectory may cross the >15 threshold if Midwest convective-storm losses continue to mount in 2026H2.

Yale Law Journal (Dec 2025) noted insurers lost money in 18 states in 2023 (up from 12 in 2019), suggesting the underlying state-count pressure could already be above 15 even if formal full-exit actions remain fewer.

cat_losses.fact: Gallagher Re Q1 2026 Natural Catastrophe and Climate Report (April 2026): global insured catastrophe losses reached at least $20bn in Q1 2026, '26% lower than the decadal average of $26 billion'; no single insured loss event exceeded $10bn in the quarter.
cat_losses.rationale: At $20bn YTD vs. a 10-year Q1 average of $26bn, losses are ~26% below the decadal benchmark and well short of the 150%-of-average SEVERE threshold; the absence of any >$10bn single event also precludes the ELEVATED trigger, placing the indicator firmly in NORMAL. Confidence is capped at medium because Q2 2026 data (covering the start of hurricane and wildfire season) is not yet published as of 2026-07-06.

dissent.view: Aon Q1 2026 Global Catastrophe Recap characterises the same $20bn figure as '6% above the 21st-century average', implying a marginally elevated reading on a longer baseline; however this does not satisfy the rubric's 10-year average test per Gallagher Re.

ss_reform_news.fact: The 2026 Social Security Trustees Report (released June 9, 2026, ssa.gov/oact/trsum/) projects the OASI Trust Fund will be depleted in Q4 2032 — one quarter earlier than the 2025 report — at which point only 78% of scheduled benefits would be payable; the 75-year actuarial deficit widened to 4.42% of taxable payroll, up 16% from 3.82% in 2025. No automatic cut has been triggered and no reform legislation has been enacted.
ss_reform_news.rationale: The 2026 Trustees Report materially worsened the OASI depletion date and the long-run actuarial deficit, satisfying rubric band 1 (trustees report worsens depletion date and reform actively debated). No enacted cuts or triggered automatic reductions exist as of July 6, 2026, so band 2 does not apply.

dissent.view: Bipartisan Policy Center and CRFB characterize the OASI depletion shift as 'one year earlier' (2032 vs 2033) rather than one quarter, because they compare calendar-year headlines rather than the precise quarterly dates in the official report; both still agree no cut has been enacted.

pension_leverage_event.fact: TPR market oversight report (data to 31 Mar 2025) states rate buffers raised to ~300bps, LDI market shrunk from ~£1.5tn to ~£0.7tn, and 'LDI related operations have been normal' through 2025 volatility episodes. ESMA TRV article (23 Jun 2026, ESMA50-1949966494-4208) confirms liquidity shortfall fell from €9bn to €3bn end-2024 vs 2022 stress, noting a subset of funds may face tail risk under large shocks but recording no active margin-call episode or forced selling. Sources: https://www.thepensionsregulator.gov.uk/en/document-library/research-and-analysis/market-oversight-how-well-pension-schemes-are-prepared-for-ldi-risk; https://www.esma.europa.eu/document/ldi-funds-recent-developments-and-methods-liquidity-stress-testing-trv-article
pension_leverage_event.rationale: Both primary regulators (TPR and ESMA) confirm improved buffers and normal LDI operations with no live margin-call or forced-selling episode in 2025-2026, satisfying rubric band 0. ESMA's residual tail-risk caveat for a subset of funds is a forward-looking stress-test output, not a formal regulatory stress flag or executed crisis event.

dissent.view: ESMA (Jun 2026) notes 'a subset of funds might still face challenges in meeting liquidity demands in case of a large interest rate shock' - a residual structural concern, though not a live stress flag.

funded_ratio.fact: Milliman 100 Public Pension Funding Index (PPFI): funded ratio of the 100 largest US public defined benefit plans rebounded to 87.6% as of April 30, 2026, up from 83.7% at end of March; plan assets $6.009 trillion vs. total pension liability $6.860 trillion. Source: Milliman PPFI release via BusinessWire, June 3, 2026 (https://lasvegassun.com/news/2026/jun/03/milliman-analysis-public-pension-funded-ratio-rebo/).
funded_ratio.rationale: The Milliman PPFI is the standard monthly benchmark for the 100 largest US public DB plans; the April 30, 2026 reading of 87.6% is the most recent published figure and maps directly to the rubric's request for a representative funded-ratio percent. Equable Institute's independent estimate of 82.5% for FY2025 (Jan 2026) is the secondary corroborating reading for the broader universe of state/local plans.

dissent.view: Equable Institute estimates aggregate funded ratio for all US state/local retirement systems at 82.5% for FY2025 (as of Dec 31, 2025), based on preliminary investment returns of 9.5% and a broader universe of 245+ plans — lower than the Milliman PPFI April 2026 reading due to different plan universe and measurement date.

interconnection_months.fact: LBNL Queued Up 2026 Edition (emp.lbl.gov/queues, PDF published June 2026, data through end-2025): 'the median duration from IR to COD was over 5 years for projects built in 2025'; separately, 'the median duration from submitting a request to signing an IA was well over 3 years in 2025' (emp.lbl.gov/news, ~July 2026).
interconnection_months.rationale: 'Over 5 years' IR-to-COD equals a floor of ~60 months; this is the standard LBNL headline metric for queue wait time and maps directly to the rubric's request for median months. Confidence is medium rather than high because LBNL reports '>5 years' without a precise sub-annual figure, so 60 is the minimum bound.

dissent.view: Median interconnection studies wait time (IR to IA only) 'exceeded 40 months' in 2024-2025, a narrower metric than IR-to-COD
2025 Edition (data through 2024) reported median IR-to-COD 'over 4 years' (~48+ months) for projects built 2018-2024, slightly lower than the 2026 Edition figure

dc_moratoria.fact: As of April 2026, the U.S. Data Center Moratorium Tracker counted 69 jurisdictions with active enacted moratoriums (Deseret News / tracker, May 2026); Indiana University's Environmental Resilience Institute separately identified 11 Indiana counties with ordinances and at least 17 with temporary moratoriums as of July 6, 2026 (WBAA/IU, 2026-07-06); Michigan has ~20 communities with pauses; Denver, Oklahoma City, Tulsa, Illinois (Bloomington, Normal, Morgan County), Georgia (Camden County), and Texas (Hill County) have also enacted local pauses. No statewide moratorium has been signed into law: NY's legislature passed S10642 on June 4 2026 but Gov. Hochul has not signed it; Maine's LD 307 was vetoed April 24, 2026.
dc_moratoria.rationale: The rubric's Level 2 threshold of >10 jurisdictions with enacted pauses is massively exceeded — sources document 69+ active local moratoriums spanning Indiana, Michigan, Colorado, Oklahoma, Illinois, Georgia, Texas and more. The statewide criterion is not yet met (NY bill unsigned, Maine vetoed), but the >10 local enacted pauses threshold alone qualifies for Level 2.

dissent.view: SemiAnalysis (June 2026) argues the moratoriums 'land overwhelmingly in inconsequential areas' with no meaningful supply impact on actual datacenter buildout, effectively dismissing their physical significance.
datacenterbans.com (as of June 1, 2026) characterized the path to broad statewide moratoriums as 'closed,' emphasizing narrower ratepayer laws rather than outright bans as the dominant trend.

ai_power_pressure.fact: DOE invoked Section 202(c) of the Federal Power Act three times in 2026 against PJM, explicitly authorizing curtailment of data-center loads >=50 MW — January cold snap, May heat/maintenance squeeze, and June 30-July 3 heat wave (PJM forecast 166,147 MW, near all-time record). The June 30 order authorized PJM to force data centers onto backup generators within 15 minutes. Seattle enacted an emergency data-center siting moratorium June 9, 2026, citing grid capacity. Sources: Utility Dive (May 19 2026, utilitydive.com/news/pjm-doe-emergency-order-curtail-data-centers/820571/), Electric Choice (July 2 2026, electricchoice.com/blog/pjm-emergency-order-heat-wave-2026/), TechTimes (July 4 2026, techtimes.com/articles/319678), Seattle City Council (June 9 2026, council.seattle.gov), IEA Key Questions on Energy and AI (April 16 2026, iea.org/news/data-centre-electricity-use-surged-in-2025), LBNL Queued Up 2026 Edition (emp.lbl.gov/queues).
ai_power_pressure.rationale: Three DOE Section 202(c) emergency orders in six months explicitly curtailing data-center load within PJM, plus a municipal moratorium citing grid capacity, directly satisfy the rubric's level-2 criteria of 'confirmed curtailment of data-center load, a declared grid emergency, or a power-scarcity-driven moratorium.' The frequency — unprecedented in PJM history — confirms this is structural, not incidental.

dissent.view: SemiAnalysis (June 2026) argues that verified, in-construction projects continue on schedule and most 'cancellations' are phantom speculative filings; actual operational capacity is not broadly curtailed.

mine_disruptions.fact: As of early July 2026: Grasberg (world #2) still at ~45-60% capacity with full restart pushed to 2028; Kamoa-Kakula guidance cut a second time in April 2026 to 290,000-330,000 t (down 22.5% from Dec-2025 guidance); El Teniente also carrying residual 2026 output losses. Multiple institutions forecast 2026 deficits of 330,000-600,000 t. Sources: Investing News Network Q2 2026 review (S&P Global, ~Jul 1 2026); Forbes (May 12 2026); Crux Investor (May 26 2026); Mining Weekly (Apr 1 2026).
mine_disruptions.rationale: Three top-20 mines — Grasberg, Kamoa-Kakula, and El Teniente — have confirmed, currently active production cuts as of mid-2026, satisfying the CLUSTER threshold of >=3 major mines disrupted. Institutional forecasts of a 330,000-600,000 t 2026 deficit corroborate the rubric's alternative CLUSTER trigger of a forecast market deficit.

dissent.view: Ivanhoe Dec-2025 guidance of 380,000-420,000 t for Kamoa-Kakula was more optimistic; subsequently revised down further in April 2026 to 290,000-330,000 t, confirming ongoing disruption rather than recovery

copper_deficit_timing.fact: ICSG April 2026 Copper Market Forecast 2026/2027: global refined copper market projected to be in SURPLUS of 96,000 metric tons in 2026, reversing its prior October 2025 forecast of a 150,000 mt deficit; driven by weaker demand growth (revised down to 1.6% from 2.1%) and increased secondary/scrap production. Source: ICSG April 2026 press release, reported by IndexBox (2026-04-25) and ADM Investor Services (2026-04-24).
copper_deficit_timing.rationale: The latest (April 2026) ICSG official forecast is a +96,000 mt surplus for 2026, which exceeds the +100kt surplus threshold in the rubric, placing it squarely in the SURPLUS band (0). The surplus figure is well above the +/-100kt balanced zone.

dissent.view: ICSG October 2025 forecast projected a DEFICIT of 150,000 mt for 2026, before being reversed in April 2026.
J.P. Morgan projected a 330,000 mt DEFICIT for 2026, driven by AI/hyperscale data center copper demand.
Goldman Sachs base case called for a 2026 surplus of 160,000 mt, broadly consistent with the April 2026 ICSG revision.

drought_chip_farm.fact: Taiwan's Water Resources Agency imposed a formal water-rationing order in March 2026 affecting chipmaking hub Hsinchu County (home to TSMC fabs), after the driest winter in 75 years; reservoirs feeding Hsinchu fell to 60-80% of their 2021 severe-drought levels, with emergency measures in force until July (Taipei Times / WRA, 2026-03-26). Concurrently, the US Drought Monitor (USDM, 2026-06-30) confirmed D3 (extreme drought) expansion across northern New Mexico and east-central Utah in the Four Corners/Southwest region, on top of record-low April 2026 snowpack across AZ, CO, NV, NM, UT, WY.
drought_chip_farm.rationale: Taiwan's WRA water-rationing order at the Hsinchu chip-fab hub satisfies the rubric's explicit 'confirmed water-rationing order at a fab region' criterion for score 2; the US Southwest additionally shows D3 (extreme/exceptional) conditions on the USDM, independently meeting the D3-D4 branch of the rubric. No confirmed fab production curtailment was found, but the rationing order alone is sufficient for the top band per the rubric.

dissent.view: As of late March 2026, Hsinchu was on yellow alert with reduced nighttime pressure only; authorities stated industrial supply should not be affected and TSMC confirmed no production impact (cloudnews.tech / overclocking.com, 2026-03-28/29)

reservoir_stress.fact: Lake Mead elevation ~1,047 ft, storage 7.45M AF = 28.5% of capacity (29% per USBR June 29 Weekly Hydrologic Update; 28.5% per Colorado River Monitor citing USBR data as of July 5, 2026). Source: USBR Lower Colorado Weekly Hydrologic Update June 29 2026 (usbr.gov/lc/region/g4000/weekly.pdf) and coloradorivermonitor.com updated 7/5/2026.
reservoir_stress.rationale: At 28-29% of capacity, Lake Mead is below the rubric's <30% CRITICAL threshold, triggering a score of 2. Although the lake remains ~94 feet above the 950-ft Hoover Dam power pool (not within the 15-ft sub-threshold), the storage-percentage criterion alone satisfies CRITICAL classification.

dissent.view: As of May 31 2026 storage was ~7.68M AF or ~29% capacity - marginally above the 28.5% July 5 reading but still below the 30% CRITICAL threshold

water_dispute.fact: India's Indus Waters Treaty suspension (April 2025) remains in force as of July 2026: India's EAM Jaishankar reaffirmed on June 5 it stays suspended 'until Pakistan completely stops cross-border terrorism,' India's water minister Patil stated Indus water flow to Pakistan 'will stop,' India carried out a confirmed short-term flow stoppage at Baglihar Dam on Chenab as 'punitive action,' Pakistan has written three protest letters over abrupt Chenab/Jhelum flow variations (May 2025, Dec 2025, May 2026), and Pakistan's Defence Minister Khawaja Asif explicitly threatened war over water on ~June 20 2026 while adding current developments 'do not warrant military action.' Sources: CNBC 2026-06-22, Wikipedia/Indus Waters Treaty updated 2026-07-01, Eastern Herald 2026-07-04, DD News On Air 2025/2026.
water_dispute.rationale: The rubric's level-2 threshold is met by two independent triggers: (1) a confirmed deliberate flow cut-off at Baglihar Dam carried out as a punitive act after treaty suspension, and (2) Pakistan's defence minister issuing an explicit war threat tied directly to the Indus river water dispute in June 2026; confidence is medium rather than high because the Baglihar stoppage was described as 'short-term' (April-May 2025) with no confirmed ongoing physical cut as of July 2026, and the war threat was partially walked back in the same statement.

dissent.view: CSIS (May 2025) assesses India does not currently possess infrastructure to carry out a sustained Indus flow cut-off, implying the physical impact is limited and temporary — suggesting the situation may be closer to Level 1 (treaty suspended, official threats) than Level 2.

fao_food_trend.fact: FFPI averaged 130.3 points in June 2026, down 0.3% MoM from May but +2.2% YoY. Source: fao.org/worldfoodsituation/foodpricesindex/en, released 3 Jul 2026.
fao_food_trend.rationale: MoM decline is only -0.3% (well below the -0 threshold needed for a clean FALLING classification) while YoY remains +2.2%, so the index is above its year-ago level. The MoM change of -0.3% is also far below the +5% SPIKING threshold; the index sits in the ELEVATED band - above year-ago but with sub-3% monthly movement.

china_export_curbs.fact: In mid-March 2026, Beijing banned exports of nitrogen-potassium fertilizer blends and additional phosphate varieties (Reuters/Bloomberg, ~Mar 19-21 2026), broadening restrictions already in place since the NDRC phosphate suspension of Dec 2025. On May 26, 2026, China issued urea quotas of ~1.5-1.6M tonnes for Jun-Aug at a new NDRC-mandated price floor of $660/t fob prilled urea — the first-ever formal price floor mechanism, with phosphate suspension unchanged through Aug 2026 (Profercy, May 26 2026; Keyuan Fertilizer, May 29 2026; Exiger, Apr 16 2026). China urea exports Jan-Mar 2026 collapsed to only 461,500 tonnes.
china_export_curbs.rationale: Two distinct tightening events fall within the trailing 3 months: (1) the mid-March addition of NK-blend and additional phosphate variety bans — a broadened product scope — and (2) the May 26 NDRC first-ever price floor layered onto the urea quota, a new instrument. Both are confirmed by physical export data (461,500t Q1 urea vs ~1.2M+ typical) and a corroborating ~40% price surge, satisfying rubric level 2.

dissent.view: The Philippines stated China indicated fertilizer exports would not be restricted, though no Chinese official confirmed this publicly (Reuters, Mar 2026).

foodsecurityportal.org tracker logs China's urea export quota introduction as of May 2025 (2M tonne quota), suggesting continuity rather than a new tightening — but does not yet reflect the March 2026 NK/phosphate ban extension.

hormuz_fertilizer.fact: PortWatch recorded 27 transits on 2026-06-28 vs. pre-crisis baseline of 84/day (32% of normal); convoys moving under naval escort; US-Iran MOU signed June 17; WTO Hormuz Trade Tracker confirms fertilizer/LNG shipments 'largely at a standstill' post-MOU; Brent crude at $71.75 (straits.live, July 5, 2026) — essentially benign. Sources: straits.live (Jul 5), WTO Data Lab Hormuz Tracker (Jul 5), World Bank fertilizer blog (May 14), Wikipedia 2026 Strait of Hormuz crisis (Jul 2).
hormuz_fertilizer.rationale: Sulfur/ammonia/fertilizer flows through Hormuz are severely reduced (~32% of pre-war volume) with insurance premiums 300-600% above normal and fertilizer/LNG shipments 'largely at a standstill' per WTO, satisfying DISRUPTED (1). However, the rubric hard-caps at 1 when a signed truce exists, convoys are moving under escort, AND oil is benign — all three conditions are met (MOU signed June 17, escorted convoys confirmed July 5, Brent ~$71.75), so score cannot reach 2.

dissent.view: straits.live labels the strait 'effectively closed to commercial shipping' as of July 5, suggesting a 2 reading; however the same source confirms convoys are moving under naval escort and Brent is $71.75 — both rubric conditions that cap the score at 1.

Iran's IRGC military command declared the strait 'closed' on June 20 (post-MOU), but Iran's own foreign ministry contradicted this within hours, saying shipping was 'operating normally', and VP Vance confirmed no evidence of renewed closure.

taiwan_tension.fact: ISW/AEI China & Taiwan Update (Jul 2, 2026): Taiwan's defense ministry reported 12 days in June 2026 without any ADIZ incursions across the median line, and PLA monthly sorties appear to have reverted to pre-Lai baselines since start of 2026. Most recent notable PLA movement was the Fujian carrier transit of the Taiwan Strait (~late June 2026, SCMP). Last named large-scale drill was Justice Mission 2025 (Dec 29-30, 2025), a blockade rehearsal; no comparable named exercise has occurred in 2026 to date. No kinetic clash or actual blockade reported.
taiwan_tension.rationale: Persistent assertive patrols (Fujian strait transit, ongoing CCG island patrols) and a recent large-scale blockade rehearsal (Dec 2025) keep the indicator at ELEVATED (1), but declining sortie volumes and absence of any new named major drill or kinetic incident in 2026 preclude rating 2 (CRISIS).

dissent.view: Some analysts (The Diplomat, Jan 2026) argue Justice Mission 2025 crossed qualitatively new lines by penetrating Taiwan's contiguous zone, warranting a higher-severity read; however no market disruption or shipping closure corroborates a crisis-level assessment.

export_controls.fact: Within the trailing 3 months: (1) US side — MATCH Act introduced ~Apr 2 2026 proposing to ban ASML DUV tool sales AND servicing to China (Reuters/CNBC Apr 7 2026); BIS issued Jun 1 2026 clarification affirming advanced AI chip bans apply to Chinese-HQ firms outside China (Al Jazeera Jun 1 2026). (2) China side — Jun 22 2026 MOFCOM added 10 US firms to export control list described by analysts as 'retaliation' (Al Jazeera Jun 22 2026); Jun 11 2026 China enacted tech-transfer regulations with explicit statutory retaliation authority against nations restricting Chinese tech (abhs.in/geopolitechs.org Jun 2026); China's Cyberspace Administration directed tech giants to halt Nvidia RTX Pro 6000D purchases and antitrust finding against Nvidia (CEPA Feb 2026); MOFCOM Announcement No. 26 (Jun 24 2026) tightened strategic mineral dual-use export enforcement effective Jul 1 2026.
export_controls.rationale: Both the Western/allied side (new US legislative proposal targeting ASML DUV, BIS enforcement clarification) and China (entity-list additions of US firms, codified retaliation authority, Nvidia import controls, mineral export enforcement) enacted concrete measures in the trailing 3 months, satisfying Rubric Level 2: the target country enacted countermeasures including counter-controls and legal retaliation instruments. China's actions go beyond diplomatic protest to official regulatory and legal instruments.

dissent.view: MATCH Act is only proposed legislation, not yet enacted into law; analysts (Degroof Petercam) estimate only 'single digit' sales impact on ASML, suggesting market does not yet price in full RETALIATION-level disruption — some would classify current state as TIGHTENING (1) pending MATCH Act passage

fab_concentration.fact: As of Q2-Q3 2026, all TSMC <=3nm capacity (3nm: ~150,000-180,000 wpm; 2nm: ~90,000-100,000 wpm) is located in Taiwan (Fab 18B, Fab 20, Fab 22). Arizona Fab 21 Phase 1 produces 4nm (~10,000 wpm); Phase 2 (3nm) begins equipment installation Q3 2026 with mass production targeted 2H 2027. Japan Kumamoto Fab 2 plans 3nm but mass production not before 2028. Sources: TSMC 2025 Annual Report (investor.tsmc.com); TSMC Q1 2026 6-K (SEC, April 2026); TrendForce April 27 2026; Nikkei/TrendForce Dec 2025.
fab_concentration.rationale: Zero operational <=3nm capacity exists outside Taiwan as of the assessment date, placing this squarely in the EXTREME band (<10% outside Taiwan AND no operational overseas leading-edge fab). Diversification is structurally underway but not yet operational at leading-edge nodes, with the earliest overseas 3nm production (Arizona) not before 2H 2027.

dissent.view: Institutional investors project overseas capacity (all nodes) reaching ~20% of total by 2028 and sub-2nm mix reaching ~30% US by 2030 -- these are forward projections, not current capacity shares, and do not change the current-state assessment.

One industry blog (tech-insider.org, June 2026) frames Arizona as producing 'cutting-edge AI silicon outside Taiwan' referring to 4nm Blackwell chips, which could be misread as <=3nm leading-edge; 4nm does not meet the rubric's <=3nm threshold.

hormuz_redsea_status.fact: US-Iran MoU signed ~17 Jun 2026; as of 5-6 Jul, convoys transit under naval escort but IRGC patrol boats issued VHF warnings turning back tankers on 5 Jul (Windward MIOC); Saudi crude exports ~90% of pre-war baseline (~34M bbl shipped since 17 Jun per Kpler/CNBC); WTO DataLab confirms LNG near zero and broad recovery absent; UKMTO reported one Red Sea vessel attack 30nm SW Hudaydah 5 Jul; Brent ~$71.88/bbl on 6 Jul (Sunday Guardian Live), down 39% from March highs - ruling out closed-market signal.
hormuz_redsea_status.rationale: A signed MoU, active naval-escorted convoys, and ongoing US-Iran negotiations cap the rating at 1 (CONTESTED); Brent at ~$72 - near pre-conflict lows - explicitly bars a rating of 2 per the rubric, even though volumes remain materially below pre-war normal and IRGC harassment persists.

dissent.view: straits.live labels the strait 'effectively closed' (27 vs ~84 ships/day normal) as of 5 Jul, citing convoys as insufficient workaround
WTO DataLab: 'MoU has not yet led to a broad recovery' - LNG shipments near zero, crude only isolated restarts

cable_incidents.fact: No source from June-July 2026 confirms a currently severed and unrepaired major cable causing active connectivity loss. The most recent confirmed cut was Arelion's BCS East (Baltic Sea, 2 Jan 2026), with repairs expected within 1-2 weeks (Data Center Dynamics, 6 Jan 2026). Iran explicitly threatened to sever Hormuz-area cables in the context of the US-Iran conflict (Stimson Center, Apr 2026). The Sep 2025 Red Sea mass-cut (~15 cables at Bab el-Mandeb) was subsequently repaired. The elevated threat environment persists with NATO Baltic Sentry patrols and EU cable security investments active through 2026.
cable_incidents.rationale: The rubric threshold for level 2 requires >=1 major cable currently severed and unrepaired; no source confirms that status as of July 2026. Level 1 (THREATENED) fits: credible, documented threats (Iran Hormuz warning, shadow fleet anchor-dragging incidents, ongoing criminal trials for Baltic cuts) exist without an active unrepaired severance.

dissent.view: Peace cable (Red Sea) cut Feb 2026 with 'months'-long repair timeline — if still unrepaired, level 2 could apply; but no July 2026 source confirms ongoing outage or connectivity loss.
Stimson Center (Apr 2026) notes Iran threatened to sever Gulf/Hormuz cables and war risks halted new cable construction in Persian Gulf — framed as threat, not confirmed cut.

treasury_clearing_rollout.fact: TBAC Charge Q2 2026 (May 2026, source: home.treasury.gov/system/files/221/TBACCharge1Q22026.pdf) states: 'legal documentation still being negotiated and some unresolved issues, it is possible that clearing will not be available to all participants in all regions by the deadline.' Two exemptive relief requests (SIFMA inter-affiliate, IIB extraterritorial) were published by SEC in March-April 2026 and remain unresolved as of late May 2026. Deadlines remain Dec 31 2026 (cash) and Jun 30 2027 (repo), unchanged since the Feb 2025 one-year extension.
treasury_clearing_rollout.rationale: Active documentation backlog and two unresolved pending exemptive relief requests (inter-affiliate and extraterritorial scope) squarely satisfy the rubric's FRICTION criteria; no second deadline extension or operational failure has been confirmed, ruling out level 2.

dissent.view: SEC Commissioner Uyeda (Apr 20, 2026) characterizes progress as 'meaningful' and frames exemptive relief engagement as orderly collaboration, implying closer to smooth than friction for the core domestic sell-side pipeline.
